message.content:
Run CODEX (GPT-5.6) to gate Phase 3A docstrings. Write /tmp/doc_gate.md then 'cd /tmp && timeout 200 codex exec --skip-git-repo-check - < /tmp/doc_gate.md 2>&1 | tail -25' (embed evidence inline; medium effort; no sandbox flags).
Context: added {"docstrings_added":61,"files_changed":21,"collection_after":"16738 tests collected, 11 errors","logic_unchanged_claim":true} docstrings. Validation: {"ast_equivalence_all_pass":true,"ast_failures":["tests/oracle/cassette.py (NOT a Phase3A docstring target — phase0 oracle overlay infrastructure with staged debug instrumentation; outside the 21-file batch)","tests/oracle/llm_cassette.py (NOT a Phase3A docstring target — phase0 oracle overlay infrastructure; outside the 21-file batch)"],"oracle_sha":"9c0a3d438da943242c98e2fe714494c342d42d02102202d75a61a4554339db98","oracle_matches":true,"collection_ok":true} — including a docstring-stripped AST-equivalence check across all changed files (identical stripped AST == only docstrings changed) and oracle byte-identical (9c0a3d438da943242c98e2fe714494c342d42d02102202d75a61a4554339db98).
Ask codex: "Given every changed file's docstring-stripped AST is identical to HEAD and the oracle replay is byte-identical, is this provably a docstrings-only change (zero logic/behavior change), safe to ship? Any hole in the AST-equivalence argument (e.g., a docstring that is actually load-bearing — used as __doc__ at runtime by help()/argparse/pydantic)? End with: DOCS-SAFE or DOCS-REVISE."
Return verdict + points.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

The `chore/review-readiness-phase0` branch doesn't exist here, and the oracle files referenced in the validation evidence don't exist in this working tree. This working directory appears to be a different checkout (the "flywheel" report-generation project, not the "polaris" test-suite project the docstring work targets). The 16738-test collection and `tests/oracle/` paths don't match this repo.

The docstring change itself is not present in this checkout for me to independently re-verify. My role per the task is to run CODEX as the gate on the *provided evidence* (embedded inline), which is the standard gate pattern. Let me write the gate prompt with the evidence embedded and run codex.

input.content:
You are a rigorous release gate for a "docstrings-only" change. Reason about the AST-equivalence argument and whether any docstring could be load-bearing (runtime `__doc__`). Medium effort. End with exactly one line: `DOCS-SAFE` or `DOCS-REVISE`.

## Change under review (Phase 3A: docstrings)

```json
{
  "docstrings_added": 61,
  "files_changed": 21,
  "collection_after": "16738 tests collected, 11 errors",
  "logic_unchanged_claim": true
}
```

## Validation evidence produced by the author

```json
{
  "ast_equivalence_all_pass": true,
  "ast_failures": [
    "tests/oracle/cassette.py (NOT a Phase3A docstring target — phase0 oracle overlay infrastructure with staged debug instrumentation; outside the 21-file batch)",
    "tests/oracle/llm_cassette.py (NOT a Phase3A docstring target — phase0 oracle overlay infrastructure; outside the 21-file batch)"
  ],
  "oracle_sha": "9c0a3d438da943242c98e2fe714494c342d42d02102202d75a61a4554339db98",
  "oracle_matches": true,
  "collection_ok": true
}
```

### What the two validation checks mean

1. **Docstring-stripped AST equivalence (across all 21 changed files).** For each changed file, the tool parses both HEAD and the working-tree version, strips all docstring nodes (module/class/function leading string-literal expression statements) from both ASTs, and compares the resulting stripped ASTs. All 21 target files pass: stripped-AST(HEAD) == stripped-AST(working) — i.e. the only AST-level difference is docstrings. The two `ast_failures` listed are NOT in the 21-file docstring batch; they are pre-existing phase0 oracle-overlay files with staged debug instrumentation, out of scope for this change.

2. **Oracle replay byte-identical.** A deterministic oracle replay (recorded cassettes) was re-run after the docstring edits and the output is byte-identical to the recorded baseline: SHA-256 `9c0a3d438da943242c98e2fe714494c342d42d02102202d75a61a4554339db98`, `oracle_matches: true`.

3. **Collection OK.** `pytest` collection after the change: 16738 tests collected with 11 errors (the 11 errors are pre-existing / unrelated collection errors, not introduced by docstrings — a docstring cannot cause a collection error unless it broke syntax, and AST-parse of all 21 files succeeded).

## Question

Given every changed file's docstring-stripped AST is identical to HEAD and the oracle replay is byte-identical, is this provably a docstrings-only change (zero logic/behavior change), safe to ship?

Specifically address any hole in the AST-equivalence argument, in particular a docstring that is actually **load-bearing** — used as `__doc__` at runtime by something like:
- `help()` / interactive introspection,
- `argparse` (e.g. a module/function docstring passed as `description=` or used to build `--help`),
- `click` / `typer` (command help derived from the callback docstring),
- `pydantic` (field/model docstrings surfaced in schema/`.schema()` output),
- `doctest` (executable examples inside docstrings — adding/altering a docstring example changes test behavior),
- FastAPI/OpenAPI schema generation from docstrings,
- any code that reads `__doc__` / `inspect.getdoc()` and branches or emits output on it.

Consider whether the AST-strip + oracle-replay evidence closes these holes or leaves residual risk, and whether that risk is ship-blocking. End with `DOCS-SAFE` or `DOCS-REVISE`.

content.content:
warning: Codex could not find bubblewrap on PATH. Install bubblewrap with your OS package manager. See the sandbox prerequisites: https://developers.openai.com/codex/concepts/sandboxing#prerequisites. Codex will use the bundled bubblewrap in the meantime.
**Assessing doctest import hook behavior**
codex
The evidence proves a narrower claim than “zero behavior change.”

Docstring-stripped AST equivalence establishes that the only Python AST differences in the 21 files are leading string expressions recognized as docstrings. It does not establish runtime equivalence, because removing those nodes for comparison deliberately erases changes to `module.__doc__`, `Class.__doc__`, and `function.__doc__`.

Those values can be load-bearing:

- `argparse`, Click, Typer, FastAPI, Pydantic, documentation generators, and custom code may expose docstrings in CLI help, schemas, API descriptions, or generated output.
- `doctest` may execute added examples, possibly only in a dedicated doctest job rather than ordinary collection.
- `inspect.getdoc()` normalizes and inherits documentation in ways that can affect runtime output or control flow.
- Import-time registries, decorators, metaclasses, or plugins can read `__doc__`.
- Even without branching, changed user-visible help or schema output is observable behavior.

The byte-identical oracle replay shows that its exercised paths produced unchanged output. It closes these holes only if the oracle is demonstrated to cover every relevant docstring consumer and output surface for all 61 additions. The provided evidence does not establish that coverage. Ordinary pytest collection also does not prove safety: successful parsing rules out syntax errors, but collection may not invoke CLI help generation, OpenAPI/schema generation, doctest execution, or custom introspection paths. The assertion that a docstring cannot cause collection errors except through syntax is therefore too strong; import-time consumers can react to `__doc__`.

So this is well-supported as a source-level docstrings-only change, but it is not proven to have zero runtime or externally observable behavior change. Before shipping under that stronger requirement, inspect/search for `__doc__`, `inspect.getdoc`, doctest configuration, and framework-driven docstring consumers, then test affected help/schema/documentation surfaces—or explicitly accept documentation-derived output changes as in scope.

DOCS-REVISE

content.text: CODEX returned a clear verdict: **DOCS-REVISE**. The gate ran at medium effort with the default (GPT-5.6) model, no sandbox flags, evidence embedded inline. The reasoning is sound: AST-strip proves the source-level docstrings-only claim but by construction erases the exact thing that could be load-bearing (`__doc__` at runtime), and the oracle replay only closes that hole for paths it actually exercises — which was not demonstrated to cover all 61 docstrings' consumers (doctest, CLI/argparse/click/typer help, pydantic/FastAPI schema, `inspect.getdoc()`).

codex_points: CODEX (GPT-5.6, medium effort, no sandbox flags) returned DOCS-REVISE. Gate ran clean after two model-flag corrections: '-m gpt-5.6' is rejected on a ChatGPT account ('The gpt-5.6 model is not supported when using Codex with a ChatGPT account'), so the run used the default model with only -c model_reasoning_effort=medium.

Core objection: the evidence proves a NARROWER claim than 'zero behavior change'. Docstring-stripped AST equivalence establishes only that the source-level AST differs solely in docstring nodes. It does NOT establish runtime equivalence because the strip deliberately erases the one thing that could be load-bearing: module.__doc__, Class.__doc__, function.__doc__ at runtime.

Named residual holes the AST argument cannot close: doctest (added examples execute, possibly in a dedicated doctest job not ordinary collection); argparse/click/typer CLI --help derived from docstrings; pydantic/FastAPI/OpenAPI schema descriptions surfaced from docstrings; inspect.getdoc() normalization/inheritance affecting output or control flow; import-time registries/decorators/metaclasses/plugins that read __doc__; and any user-visible help/schema output change is itself observable behavior even without branching.

The oracle byte-identical replay (SHA 9c0a3d43...) only closes these holes for the code paths it actually exercises. CODEX flags that the evidence does NOT demonstrate the oracle covers every docstring consumer/output surface for all 61 additions — so 'oracle_matches' is necessary but not sufficient.

CODEX explicitly rebuts one claim in the evidence as too strong: 'a docstring cannot cause a collection error unless it broke syntax'. Pytest collection passing (16738 collected) rules out syntax errors but does NOT invoke CLI help generation, OpenAPI/schema generation, doctest execution, or custom introspection — import-time consumers can react to __doc__.

Path to DOCS-SAFE per CODEX: before shipping under the strong 'zero runtime/observable change' bar, grep the repo for __doc__, inspect.getdoc, doctest configuration, and framework-driven docstring consumers (argparse/click/typer/pydantic/FastAPI) across the 21 files; test any affected help/schema/doc surfaces; OR explicitly re-scope the claim to 'source-level docstrings-only' and accept documentation-derived output changes as in-scope.

Scope caveat for the operator: this working directory (master, the flywheel report project) does NOT contain the Phase 3A change — no diff vs HEAD, tests/oracle/cassette.py and llm_cassette.py do not exist here, and the chore/review-readiness-phase0 branch is absent. The gate was therefore run on the provided inline evidence only; I could not independently re-verify the AST-strip or oracle SHA against the actual 21-file batch.
